question: True or False? If ($x_n$) is bounded and diverges, then there exist two subsequences of ($x_n$) that converge to different limits. Answer True or False.

conversation.content: True or False? If ($x_n$) is bounded and diverges, then there exist two subsequences of ($x_n$) that converge to different limits. Answer True or False.

True.

A bounded sequence has at least one convergent subsequence (Bolzano–Weierstrass). If all convergent subsequences had the same limit L, then the whole sequence would converge to L, contradicting divergence. Hence there must be at least two subsequences converging to different limits.

then the answer: True

This does not look right to me—could you re-check your work?  End with a final line exactly like:
  then the answer: <final answer>
- Put nothing after that final line

True. If every convergent subsequence had the same limit L, then any epsilon>0 leaves at most finitely many terms outside (L-epsilon,L+epsilon); otherwise one could extract a subsequence outside that interval which by Bolzano–Weierstrass has a convergent subsequence with limit ≠ L, contradiction. Hence x_n→L, contradicting divergence; so there must be two subsequences with different limits.

then the answer: True

True. By Bolzano–Weierstrass a bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence with limit L. If every convergent subsequence had limit L, then for any ε>0 only finitely many terms could lie outside (L−ε,L+ε) (otherwise one could extract a subsequence outside that interval and, by boundedness, a convergent subsequence with limit ≠L), so x_n→L, contradicting divergence. Hence there are at least two subsequences with different limits.

True. By Bolzano–Weierstrass a bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence with limit L. If every convergent subsequence had limit L, then for some ε>0 infinitely many terms outside (L−ε,L+ε) would yield, by boundedness, a convergent subsequence with limit ≠L, contradiction; hence not all convergent subsequences can share the same limit, so there exist two subsequences with different limits.

True. By Bolzano–Weierstrass, a bounded sequence has at least one convergent subsequence with limit L. If no subsequence converged to a limit different from L, then for every ε>0 only finitely many terms lie outside (L−ε,L+ε) (otherwise those infinitely many terms, being bounded, would yield by Bolzano–Weierstrass a subsequence converging to a different limit), hence x_n→L, contradicting divergence. Therefore there must exist two subsequences with different limits.
